data.title: Block changes ticker symbol to XYZ
data.description:
• Block (NYSE:SQ) said on Thursday that it will be changing its ticker symbols from “SQ” and “SQ2” to “XYZ”.
  • This is in connection with the company’s earlier name change to “Block” and its ecosystem of brands including Cash App, Square, Afterpay and TIDAL.
  • The change will be implemented across the company’s dual listings on the New York Stock Exchange and the Australian Securities Exchange.
  • Effective January 21, 2025, the company’s Class A common stock will trade on the NYSE under the new symbol “XYZ”. Effective January 22, 2025, the company’s ticker code on the ASX will also change to “XYZ”.

data.title: Block to change ticker symbol to 'XYZ'
data.description:
Block (NYSE:SQ) will change its ticker symbol to "XYZ" from "SQ" and SQ2", reflecting its broader range of businesses beyond its original Square payment system, the company said on Thursday.

The change will become effective on Jan. 21, 2025, for shares trading on the New York Stock Exchange. The change will take effect on Jan. 22 for shares traded on the Australian Securities Exchange.

The company now includes Cash App, Afterpay, and TIDAL, in addition to its Square seller system.

Block (NYSE:SQ) will release Q4 financial results on Feb. 20, 2025.

data.title: Sweden's army plans to buy $2B in tanks from KNDS
data.description:
• The Swedish army plans to buy tanks from German-French defense contractor KNDS for 22 billion Swedish crowns ($1.97 billion), Sweden's government said in a statement Thursday.
  • The order includes 44 Leopard 2 A8 tanks and upgrades for 66 existing tanks.
  • "This is an absolutely necessary investment," Swedish Defense Minister Pal Johnson said to public broadcaster SVT. The tanks will help the country to meet its goal of doubling the size of its army, he said.
  • Deliveries of the tanks will start in 2028 and run through 2031, according to the statement.

data.title: KKR, PSP Investments to buy stake in two American Electric Power companies for $2.8B
data.description:
KKR (KKR) said Thursday it formed a 50/50 strategic partnership with Canada's PSP Investments to acquire 19.9% stakes in American Electric Power's (NASDAQ:AEP) Ohio and Indiana transmission company, as well as its Michigan transmission company, for a combined $2.82B.

The companies said their investment will support AEP's (NASDAQ:AEP) ability to meet increasing customer demand and enhance grid reliability, and support the utility's previously announced five-year capital plan to benefit customers.

AEP (AEP) said it will remain the majority owner and operator of both transmission companies.

The company, which serves 5.6M retail and wholesale customers across 11 states, said it considers Ohio, Indiana and Michigan among its fastest-growing service territories, driven by strong manufacturing industries in the states and newer sources of load growth.

"This transaction allows AEP to efficiently finance a growing segment of our business and enhances our ability to serve growing customer demand and provide reliable service to our customers," CEO Bill Fehrman said.

data.title: JLL secures $300M refinancing for Omni Nashville Hotel
data.description:
• JLL (NYSE:JLL)'s Capital Markets group on Thursday said it has arranged a $300 million refinancing for the Omni Nashville Hotel, an 800-key luxury hotel in downtown Nashville.
  • JLL worked on behalf of the borrower, TRT Holdings, to secure a fixed-rate, seven-year loan through a U.S.-based insurance company and Alliance Bernstein Commercial Real Estate Debt, in its capacity as advisor to Equitable Financial Life Insurance Company.
  • JLL +4.99% after hours to $260.33.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Pharma companies welcome new year with 583 drug price hikes so far
data.description:
Drugmakers have started 2025, implementing price hikes for 583 products within the first two days of the new year alone, 46brooklyn Research, an Ohio non-profit focused on U.S. drug pricing data, said in a report on Thursday.

Considering the drugs’ wholesale acquisition cost (WAC) or list prices, the firm said that 590 pricing changes, including 583 price hikes and seven decreases, had occurred at the start of the new year.

While nearly a third of WAC increases have occurred on Jan. 1 over the past seven years, this year witnessed more list price increases than any other year, 46brooklyn Research said, citing data from 2011.

According to its report, except for Merck’s (NYSE:MRK) diabetes therapy, Januvia, for which WAC has dropped 42%, all the drugs subject to 2026 Medicare price negotiations have seen list price increases.

They include a 3% WAC price increase implemented for Eli Lilly’s (NYSE:LLY) diabetes therapy Jardiance and a 2% increase introduced for Eliquis, a blood thinner from Bristol Myers (NYSE:BMY) and Pfizer (NYSE:PFE).

Other notable price hikes include a 3% increase for Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) GLP-1 diabetes therapy Ozempic, a 5.9% increase for Gilead’s (GILD) HIV medication Biktarvy, and an 8% hike for Roche’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) (OTCQX:RHHBF) hemophilia therapy Hemlibra.

Meanwhile, Collegium Pharma (COLL) has implemented a 15%-20% WAC price increase for Nucynta, one of the last opioids to remain on the market without generic alternatives. “This likely represents the last big opportunity for the drug manufacturer to capture whatever additional value is gained from list price changes before the product begins to cede ground to generic competitors,” 46brooklyn Research wrote.

However, its update doesn’t indicate price hikes for products manufactured by pharma majors Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY), AstraZeneca (AZN), AbbVie (ABBV), and Vertex Pharma (VRTX).

As for price drops, the firm said that pharma companies have sharply cut the prices of several asthma inhalers and central nervous system (CNS) drugs amid regulatory pushback.

For example, Boehringer Ingelheim has dropped the price of its asthma medication Atrovent HFA by 35%. In 2023, the German drugmaker, along with its rivals, such as AbbVie (ABBV), GSK (GSK), and Teva (TEVA), was subject to an FTC warning related to patent violations over asthma inhalers and other products.

data.title: Shell, Chevron start oil production at Gulf of Mexico's Whale project
data.description:
Shell (NYSE:SHEL) said Thursday it began production at its Whale floating facility in the Gulf of Mexico, which is expected to reach peak output of 100K boe/day; Whale is operator and 60% owned by Shell, with the other 40% held by Chevron (CVX).

Shell (SHEL) said Whale, which holds a recoverable resource volume of 480M boe, achieved first oil around seven years after the development was discovered, primarily due to a delay in reaching a final investment decision following the company's cash preservation strategy adopted during the COVID-19 pandemic.

The company expects its operations at Whale will have a greenhouse gas emissions intensity ~30% lower than its nearby Vito floating platform due to the use of more efficient turbines and compression systems.

After selling its onshore Permian Basin business to ConocoPhillips in recent years, Shell's only U.S. production is in the Gulf of Mexico.

Chevron (CVX) said production from the Whale development would bring it closer to reaching 300K net boe/day in the Gulf of Mexico by 2026.

data.title: Peacock president Kelly Campbell steps down
data.description:
• Kelly Campbell, the president of NBC-owned streaming service Peacock, is stepping down, according to multiple media reports from Wednesday.
  • Campbell was previously president of Hulu, where she led the SVOD and live-TV streaming businesses, following a role as its chief marketing officer.
  • Prior to that, she spent 12 years at Google in various leadership roles across the Google Ads and Google Cloud businesses. She began her career as an investment banker at JPMorgan Chase.

data.title: Amentum awarded $447M Air Forces Central Command Global Prepositioned Material Services contract
data.description:
• Amentum (NYSE:AMTM) has been awarded the $447M Air Forces Central Command Global Prepositioned Material Services (GPMS) contract to deliver and modernize prepositioned storage and maintenance solutions.
  • Amentum will leverage proven technologies for smart asset management and a customized data management system.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Elon Musk doubts DOGE can achieve $2T in U.S. budget cuts under Trump
data.description:
Elon Musk, the tech billionaire tapped for a government efficiency panel by President-elect Donald Trump, has expressed skepticism about the feasibility of cutting $2T from the U.S. federal budget, backtracking on his own previously ambitious claims.

The $2T goal was a "best-case outcome," Musk said in an interview broadcast on social media platform X with political strategist Mark Penn, noting "we've got a good shot" at getting half of that.

Reaching that figure would probably require cutting back on programs like Social Security and Medicare, which enjoy stronger voter support and which Trump promised to preserve during his campaign.

In fiscal-year 2024, out of the $6.75T in government expenditures, over $5.3T was allocated to Social Security, health care, defense, veterans' benefits and debit interest payments.

The hardest area to tackle has been popular entitlement programs like Medicare, Medicaid and Social Security, which have been major contributors to the deficit. With no easy solutions there, Trump has tasked Tesla (TSLA) CEO Musk and former Republican presidential candidate Vivek Ramaswamy to head up the newly formed Department of Government Efficiency, or DOGE, to make government operations more efficient.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
